data.question: Which of the following describes a "key person" in a business?

options.A: Any employee who has been with the company for more than 5 years
options.B: An employee whose death, incapacity, or departure would cause severe financial harm to the business
options.C: The person responsible for accounting and financial reporting
options.D: A shareholder with the largest equity stake in the company

data.explanation: A key person is an employee whose death, incapacity, or departure would cause severe financial harm to the business. This is typically someone with unique skills, client relationships, or expertise that cannot be easily replaced.

data.question: If a business has taken out a "demand loan" from a bank, what is the primary risk to the business if a key person dies?

options.A: The interest rate on the loan will increase automatically
options.B: The bank will require additional collateral before releasing any funds
options.C: The lender may demand immediate full repayment of the loan
options.D: The business will be required to replace the deceased key person within 30 days

data.explanation: A demand loan can be recalled at any time by the creditor. If the lender believes the death of a key person jeopardizes the business's ability to repay, they may exercise the right to demand immediate full repayment.

data.question: Jason and Ivan own a successful plumbing business together as equal partners. Jason dies suddenly without a written buy-sell agreement in place. What is the likely consequence?

options.A: Ivan automatically becomes the sole owner of the business
options.B: The business is automatically dissolved and assets are liquidated
options.C: Jason's heirs inherit his business share and may demand voting rights, management control, or liquidation
options.D: The business is transferred to Jason's creditors as payment of his personal debts

data.explanation: Without a buy-sell agreement, the deceased's heirs automatically inherit the business share and may demand voting rights, management control, or the right to liquidate.

data.question: Christina owns a $1.5 million garden centre and has assets totaling $2 million. She has two adult children. What is the primary challenge Christina faces in her estate planning?

options.A: The business is worth too little to be divided equally between her children
options.B: An equality challenge: the garden centre is much larger than her other assets, creating an imbalance in inheritance distribution
options.C: She will need to pay capital gains tax on the entire value of the business immediately
options.D: The garden centre cannot be transferred to family members under Canadian tax law

data.explanation: Christina faces an equality challenge: the garden center is much larger than her other assets, creating an imbalance in inheritance distribution between her two children.

data.question: What happens to the shares of a successful business owner when they die from a Canadian tax perspective?

options.A: The shares pass to the heirs tax-free with no deemed disposition
options.B: There is a deemed disposition at fair market value on the date of death, triggering capital gains inclusion
options.C: The shares are automatically cancelled and the business ceases to exist
options.D: The heirs must immediately sell the shares to the remaining shareholders

data.explanation: Upon death, Canadian tax law deems the deceased to have disposed of their shares at fair market value on the date of death. This triggers a capital gains inclusion (50% of the gain is taxable).

data.question: From a legal and tax perspective, how is a "sole proprietorship" treated when the owner dies?

options.A: The business automatically transfers to the business's named executor
options.B: The business ceases to exist as a legal entity because it is not a separate legal entity from the owner
options.C: The business continues indefinitely under the ownership of the deceased's heirs
options.D: The business is treated as a corporation and continues as a separate legal entity

data.explanation: A sole proprietorship is not a separate legal entity—the business and the owner are one and the same. Upon the owner's death, the business ceases to exist as a legal entity.

data.question: In a standard general partnership, what level of personal liability do the partners have?

options.A: Limited liability; personal assets are protected from partnership debts
options.B: Unlimited personal liability; each partner is jointly and severally liable for all partnership debts
options.C: Liability is limited to the amount each partner has invested in the partnership
options.D: No personal liability; only the partnership assets can be used to satisfy debts

data.explanation: In a general partnership, partners have unlimited personal liability. Each partner is jointly and severally liable, meaning a creditor can pursue any partner for the full amount of the partnership's debt.

data.question: Which of the following accurately describes the liability of a "limited partner" in a limited partnership?

options.A: Unlimited personal liability, similar to a general partner
options.B: Liability is capped at their investment in the partnership
options.C: No liability whatsoever for partnership debts or obligations
options.D: Liability that increases as the partnership's profits increase

data.explanation: A limited partner's liability is capped at their investment in the partnership. They cannot be held personally responsible for partnership debts beyond what they invested.

data.question: What is a key characteristic of a corporation?

options.A: A separate legal entity created by law with limited liability for shareholders
options.B: An unincorporated business structure where all owners have unlimited liability
options.C: A partnership where all partners have limited liability by default
options.D: A sole proprietorship that has been registered with the government

data.explanation: A corporation is a separate legal entity created by law. It owns assets, enters contracts, and is taxed independently of its owners. Shareholders have limited liability.

data.question: To qualify for the Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption (LCGE), the business shares must meet certain criteria. Which of the following is a requirement?

options.A: The shares must be in a publicly traded company listed on a major stock exchange
options.B: The owner must have held the shares for a minimum of 10 years
options.C: The shares must be qualified small business shares (QSBS) of a Canadian-Controlled Private Corporation
options.D: The business must have annual revenue exceeding $1 million

data.explanation: The Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption applies only to qualified small business shares (QSBS), which include shares of Canadian-Controlled Private Corporations (CCPCs) and family farm or fishing corporations.
